title: Kitten or Cat Dragging Hind Legs: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your cat is dragging its back legs, it is a medical emergency. Learn about potential causes like saddle thrombus and why you must see a vet now.
content_html:
TL;DR: Sudden hind limb paralysis in cats is a critical veterinary emergency that requires immediate medical intervention. Seek emergency care immediately to manage pain and address life-threatening causes like blood clots or spinal trauma.

What causes sudden hind limb paralysis in cats and kittens?

When a cat or kitten suddenly loses the ability to use their back legs, it is a condition known as posterior paralysis or paresis. This often happens suddenly and can be caused by various issues, including Feline Aortic Thromboembolism (also known as a saddle thrombus), spinal cord trauma, or severe neurological infections. In many cases, the cat may appear distressed, vocalize loudly, or have limbs that feel cold to the touch.

How urgent is it if my cat is unable to use their back legs?

  • This is considered a critical veterinary emergency; you should not wait for an appointment or observe them at home.
  • Immediate intervention is required to manage pain and address life-threatening causes such as blood clots or spinal compression.
  • Time is of the essence to improve the chances of recovery and prevent permanent nerve damage.

How can a video or photo help the vet triage my cat?

  • If you can safely do so without delaying your trip to the emergency room, capture a quick video of your cat attempting to move.
  • Cats often have adrenaline spikes at the clinic that can mask symptoms or alter their gait.
  • A video provides the veterinary team with a clear, objective record of the event in a home environment.
  • Visual evidence allows the vet to more accurately differentiate between neurological weakness and physical injury.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Arterial thromboembolism, often secondary to conditions causing left atrial enlargement such as cardiomyopathy, hyperthyroidism, or congenital heart disease, can result in hindlimb paresis or paralysis. Clinical signs include pain, reduced or absent arterial pulses (femoral or pedal) in the affected limbs, which are often cooler than normal with firm, swollen gastrocnemius muscles. Doppler ultrasonography can help evaluate blood flow in the distal aorta and femoral arteries. Management involves analgesics, nursing care, and treatment of any underlying disease, with anticoagulants such as heparin used to reduce further thrombus formation. Neurologic deficits may improve over weeks to months, but permanent deficits are possible, and the long-term prognosis remains guarded, with a high rate of mortality or euthanasia during the initial episode.

Chapter: Cardiology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 127)

faqs.question: Is dragging hind legs always a sign of pain?
faqs.answer: In most sudden cases, such as a blood clot, it is extremely painful for the cat. Even if the cat is not vocalizing, paralysis is a high-stress medical event that requires professional pain management.

faqs.question: What is a saddle thrombus?
faqs.answer: A saddle thrombus is a blood clot that lodges at the base of the aorta, cutting off blood flow to the hind legs. It is a common cause of sudden paralysis in cats and is a life-threatening emergency.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat human aspirin for the pain?
faqs.answer: No. Never administer human pain medications to a cat. Many are highly toxic to felines and can lead to organ failure or death, complicating their emergency treatment.
